blog_articles.title: Easing Math Anxiety in Preschoolers: 7 Playful Strategies
blog_articles.summary: How to spot early signs of math anxiety in preschool and use games, stories, and routines to help young children feel calm and confident with numbers.
blog_articles.body:
Many parents are surprised to see signs of math anxiety as early as ages 3–5. A child might hide during counting games, freeze when asked “how many?”, or say “I’m bad at math” before kindergarten.

This article explains what math anxiety looks like in preschoolers, why it shows up, and how to respond in a way that protects your child’s confidence. We walk through seven playful strategies you can start today: building a predictable counting routine, using number talk during everyday activities, inviting your child to estimate rather than be exact, celebrating mistakes as part of learning, and more.

You’ll also find sample scripts for responding when your child shuts down, plus ideas for partnering with preschool teachers so home and school send the same positive messages about math.

blog_articles.title: When Numbers Feel Scary: Understanding Math Anxiety in Young Children
blog_articles.summary: A research-informed overview of math anxiety in young children, with simple checklists for parents and preschool teachers.
blog_articles.body:
Math anxiety isn’t just a “big kid” problem. In fact, some studies suggest that worries about math can begin as early as age 4. Young children may not have the words to say “I feel anxious,” so their feelings show up as avoidance, perfectionism, or sudden tummy aches during math time.

In this article, we translate the research on math anxiety into everyday language. You’ll learn what we know about where math anxiety comes from, how adult attitudes matter, and which classroom and home practices can reduce stress. We include separate checklists for parents and educators to help you notice early warning signs without labeling children.

Finally, we outline a simple 3-step response plan you can use when a child seems overwhelmed: pause and validate, shrink the task, and return to play-based exploration.

blog_articles.title: Designing Pre-K & Kindergarten Math Centers That Reduce Anxiety
blog_articles.summary: Practical tips for preschool and kindergarten teachers to design math centers that feel safe for anxious learners.
blog_articles.body:
The structure of your math centers can either calm or amplify math anxiety. In early childhood classrooms, small design choices—like how children choose tasks, how you respond to mistakes, and how materials are organized—have a big impact.

We share five design principles for anxiety-sensitive math centers: offer multiple entry points, normalize “I’m still learning” language, build in visual supports, rotate in familiar games, and prioritize collaboration over speed. Sample center cards and photo examples help you visualize changes you can make this week.

While written for educators, we also include a short section for families to adapt these ideas for at-home play stations.

blog_articles.title: Building Counting Confidence at Home for Ages 3–4
blog_articles.summary: Simple, low-prep routines that help 3- and 4-year-olds count, compare, and enjoy playing with numbers.
blog_articles.body:
You don’t need fancy materials to build strong counting skills. In this article, we show you how to turn snack time, bath time, and walks around the block into rich counting practice.

We focus on three big ideas: saying number words in order, matching numbers to objects (one-to-one correspondence), and comparing small quantities. Each idea includes quick games, example questions, and tips for adjusting the challenge level based on your child’s comfort.

blog_articles.title: Playful Patterns: Why Repeating Designs Matter in Preschool Math
blog_articles.summary: Discover how simple pattern games with blocks, beads, and sounds build a foundation for algebraic thinking.
blog_articles.body:
Patterns are more than pretty designs—they’re an early form of algebra. When children notice and extend patterns, they’re practicing prediction, reasoning, and flexible thinking.

We share a progression of pattern activities from toddlerhood through kindergarten, ideas for pattern talk, and ways to support children who struggle to see “what comes next.”

blog_articles.title: Everyday Math Talk: A Parent’s Guide for Ages 3–6
blog_articles.summary: How to weave rich math conversations into routines like cooking, laundry, and playtime—without turning life into a worksheet.
blog_articles.body:
Math talk isn’t about quizzing your child. It’s about wondering out loud, comparing, and inviting them to notice what they see. We break down the four types of math talk that matter most in early childhood and provide sentence starters you can borrow.

Short dialogue examples show what this sounds like in real life, including moments when children resist or get frustrated.

blog_articles.title: What Is Number Sense in Early Childhood?
blog_articles.summary: A plain-language explanation of number sense, with examples of what it looks like from preschool through grade 2.
blog_articles.body:
Number sense is the flexible understanding of what numbers mean and how they relate to each other. Children with strong number sense can reason about quantities without relying solely on memorized procedures.

We outline key milestones for ages 3–8 and share activities that gently stretch children’s thinking without pressure.

blog_articles.title: Supporting Math Learning in Bilingual Homes
blog_articles.summary: Strategies for families and educators to honor home languages while building strong early math skills.
blog_articles.body: Bilingual children can thrive in math when adults send clear messages that all of their languages are assets. We share tips for mixing languages during math talk, working with translated materials, and partnering with teachers around vocabulary.

blog_articles.title: Coming Soon: Summer Playful Math Camps (Preview)
blog_articles.summary: A sneak peek at upcoming small-group online and in-person math camps for ages 4–8.
blog_articles.body: We’re finalizing our summer camp schedule. This preview outlines our themes, age groups, and general structure. Full details and registration links will be published later this spring.

blog_articles.title: Math Anxiety in the Early Years: Live Q&A for Parents and Teachers
blog_articles.summary: Draft article for an upcoming webinar on math anxiety in preschool and early grades.
blog_articles.body: This draft will be updated with registration links and key questions before publication.

offerings.title: Playful Numbers Online: Ages 4–5 (Tuesday Group)
offerings.shortDescription: A 6-week small-group online math program for 4–5-year-olds, meeting Tuesdays 4:30–5:15 pm.
offerings.longDescription:
In this 6-week online small-group program, 4–5-year-olds explore early number sense, counting, and shapes through songs, stories, and hands-on games. Each live session is limited to 6 children to allow for individual attention. Families receive simple follow-up activities after each class.

Perfect for preschoolers who are curious about numbers or need a gentle confidence boost before kindergarten.

offerings.title: Number Stories Online: Ages 4–5 (Thursday Group)
offerings.shortDescription: Story-based 6-week online math circle for 4–5-year-olds, Thursdays 5:00–5:45 pm.
offerings.longDescription: Each week, children join a small online circle to hear and act out a math-rich story. We explore counting, comparing, and simple addition using puppets, movement, and everyday objects from home. Small-group size allows for turn-taking and discussion.

offerings.title: Math Play Lab: Ages 3–4 (Saturday In-Person)
offerings.shortDescription: Hands-on math playgroup for 3–4-year-olds in NYC, Saturdays 10:00–11:00 am.
offerings.longDescription: Children explore counting, shapes, and patterns using sensory bins, blocks, and music in a relaxed playgroup setting. Caregivers are welcome to stay and learn simple strategies to continue the play at home.

offerings.title: 1:1 Kindergarten Math Coaching – 4-Session Evening Package
offerings.shortDescription: Four weekly 30-minute 1:1 online coaching sessions for kindergarteners, with early evening time slots.
offerings.longDescription:
This 4-session package is designed for kindergarteners who need extra support or enrichment in early math. Each 30-minute 1:1 online session is customized to your child’s goals, such as counting to 100, adding and subtracting within 10, or building confidence.

Families choose an early evening slot, and we reserve the same time for four consecutive weeks to make scheduling simple.
skillsFocus: count and compare numbers up to 100

offerings.title: 1:1 Kindergarten Math Coaching – 8-Session Package
offerings.shortDescription: Eight weekly 30-minute 1:1 sessions for deeper skill building in kindergarten math.
offerings.longDescription: An extended coaching package for families who want consistent support across two months. Sessions focus on number sense, word problems, and flexible strategies for addition and subtraction.

offerings.title: Raising a Math-Confident Child: Parent Workshop (Ages 3–5)
offerings.shortDescription: A 90-minute in-person workshop for parents and caregivers near ZIP 10001, focused on playful math at home for ages 3–5.
offerings.longDescription:
This interactive 90-minute workshop is designed for parents and caregivers of children ages 3–5. You’ll learn how early math develops, what math anxiety can look like in young children, and simple ways to build math into everyday routines.

We’ll model games you can play with no special materials, share language to use when your child feels stuck, and leave plenty of time for questions. You’ll walk away with a printable activity pack and a clear plan for the next month.

offerings.title: Zoom Parent Session: 10-Minute Math Games for Busy Families
offerings.shortDescription: A 60-minute online parent workshop with live demos of quick math games for ages 3–7.
offerings.longDescription: Join us online to experience short, high-impact games that build counting, number sense, and early operations—without worksheets.

offerings.title: Parent Math Night: Ages 3–5 (NJ Suburbs)
offerings.shortDescription: A 90-minute in-person workshop for parents of preschoolers in New Jersey.
offerings.longDescription: In this evening session, we explore early math milestones and hands-on games you can play at home.

offerings.title: Confident Counters: Preschool Teacher PD Package
offerings.shortDescription: A professional development package for preschool teachers with 3 live coaching calls and ready-to-use counting centers.
offerings.longDescription:
This package supports preschool teams in strengthening counting and number sense instruction. It includes a 90-minute virtual workshop, three follow-up coaching calls, and a resource library of counting center plans and family handouts.

Coaching calls are spaced over 6–8 weeks to allow time for classroom try-it tasks and reflection.

offerings.title: Math Anxiety–Sensitive Teaching: Coaching Bundle for Preschool Teams
offerings.shortDescription: A specialized professional development package with 4 small-group coaching calls focused on reducing math anxiety.
offerings.longDescription: This bundle helps preschool and pre-K teachers recognize and reduce math anxiety in their classrooms. It includes a kickoff workshop, four 60-minute small-group coaching calls, observation tools, and reflection templates. Coaching calls focus on real classroom scenarios brought by participants.

offerings.title: Early Math Coaching Cycle (2 Calls)
offerings.shortDescription: A short coaching cycle with two virtual coaching calls for individual preschool teachers.
offerings.longDescription: Ideal for individual teachers or small programs looking for targeted feedback on math instruction, this package includes an initial goal-setting call and a follow-up reflection call.

offerings.title: 12-Week Number Sense & Patterns Curriculum (Ages 4–5)
offerings.shortDescription: A complete 12-week curriculum bundle focusing on number sense and patterns, with 4 lessons per week for preschool classrooms.
offerings.longDescription:
This digital curriculum bundle includes 12 weeks of lesson plans, center activities, and family extensions focused on number sense and patterns for ages 4–5. Each week features at least four ready-to-use lessons, assessment checklists, and printable materials.

Lessons are designed for whole-group, small-group, and center time, with clear prompts to support math talk and reduce math anxiety.

offerings.title: 8-Week Number Sense Booster (Kindergarten)
offerings.shortDescription: An 8-week curriculum bundle for kindergarten classrooms with 3 lessons per week.
offerings.longDescription: Designed for mid-year support, this bundle offers eight weeks of number sense lessons, including whole-group experiences and small-group practice.

offerings.title: 12-Week Shape Explorers Curriculum (Ages 3–4)
offerings.longDescription: This curriculum focuses on shapes, spatial reasoning, and early measurement for 3–4-year-olds.

offerings.title: Kindergarten Readiness Math Club (Online)
offerings.shortDescription: 6-week online math club for older preschoolers preparing for kindergarten.
offerings.longDescription: This small-group club supports children who will enter kindergarten in the fall with focused work on number sense, patterns, and problem-solving.

resources.title: Shape Hunt & Count Walk
resources.summary: Go on a short indoor or outdoor walk to find and count everyday shapes up to 10.
resources.description: Perfect for 3-year-olds, this activity turns your home or neighborhood into a shape adventure. Children look for circles, squares, and triangles, then count how many they see.
resources.instructions:
1. Draw three simple boxes on a piece of paper and label them with a circle, square, and triangle.
2. Tell your child you’re going on a Shape Hunt Walk. Ask, “How many circles can we find?”
3. Walk around your home or outside. Each time your child spots a shape, pause to name it together and add a tally mark in the matching box.
4. When you’re done, count the tallies in each box together. Ask questions like, “Which shape did we find more of? Which did we find fewer of?”
objectives: Children will practice counting up to 10 objects.
Children will identify basic shapes in their environment.
Children will compare quantities using everyday language (more/fewer).

resources.title: Snack Shape Sort & Count
resources.summary: Use snack time to sort foods by shape and count how many are in each group.
resources.description: Turn snack into a mini math lesson by sorting foods into groups by shape and counting the pieces in each group.
resources.instructions:
1. Place a small handful of snack items on a plate.
2. Show your child how to sort by shape (for example, round cereal vs. square crackers).
3. After sorting, count each group together using one-to-one pointing.
4. Ask, “Which group has more? Which has fewer? What if we eat one—how many are left?”
objectives: Children will practice sorting objects by shape.
Children will count small sets of objects accurately.

resources.title: Block Towers: Shapes & Counting
resources.summary: Build towers with blocks of different shapes and count how many blocks are in each tower.
resources.description: Use any building blocks you have at home to explore shape names and counting.
resources.instructions:
1. Invite your child to build a tower using just one shape of block (for example, all rectangles).
2. Count the blocks in the tower together. Say the last number louder to emphasize the total.
3. Build a second tower with a different shape (for example, cylinders) and count again.
4. Compare: “Which tower is taller? Which has more blocks?”
5. If your child is interested, mix shapes in one tower and talk about the shapes you see as you count.
objectives: Children will count objects in a vertical arrangement.
Children will use informal language to describe 3D shapes.

resources.title: Bath Toy Shape Scoop & Count
resources.summary: Use bath time toys to scoop, sort by shape, and count small collections.
resources.description: Make bath time a math time by scooping and sorting floating toys into groups of similar shapes and counting them.
resources.instructions:
1. Place a few different bath toys in the tub (for example, ducks, boats, and balls).
2. Ask your child to scoop up a few toys with the cup and place them on the edge of the tub.
3. Help them sort the toys by shape or type (all the balls together, all the boats together).
4. Count each group, touching each toy as you say a number.
5. Play again with a new scoop and see if the number changes.
objectives: Children will sort objects by a chosen attribute.
Children will count small sets of objects accurately.

resources.title: Shape Match & Count Card Game
resources.summary: Print simple shape cards to play a matching and counting game with your 3-year-old.
resources.description: This game uses printable shape cards or hand-drawn cards to practice matching and counting sets up to 6.
resources.instructions:
1. Print or draw pairs of simple shape cards (two circles, two squares, two triangles, etc.).
2. Shuffle the cards and lay them face up.
3. Take turns finding a matching pair of shapes. Each time you make a match, count how many shapes are in your pile.
4. For an extra challenge, flip the cards face down and play a memory game, still counting matches as you go.
objectives: Children will count the total number of matched cards.

resources.title: Toy Car Counting Races
resources.summary: Race toy cars and count how many spaces they move.
resources.description: A simple movement game where children roll a die and move cars along a homemade track, counting each space as they go.
resources.instructions: Draw a simple track with 10 boxes and use a die to decide how many spaces to move each turn. Count aloud as you move.
objectives: Children will practice one-to-one counting along a path.

resources.title: Shape Sorting Mats (Printable)
resources.summary: Printable mats for sorting household items by shape.
resources.description: Use these mats as a base for sorting buttons, blocks, or snack pieces by shape.
resources.instructions: Print the mats, place them on a table, and invite your child to place each object on the mat with the matching shape.
objectives: Children will classify objects based on shape.

resources.title: Counting to 10 Action Song
resources.summary: A short video teaching counting to 10 with movements.
resources.description: Sing and move along to practice counting to 10 with your child.
resources.instructions: Play the video and invite your child to do the actions and say the numbers along with the song.
objectives: Children will practice the verbal counting sequence to 10.

resources.title: Everyday Math at Home: Quick Guide
resources.summary: A short guide for parents with simple math ideas for ages 3–6.
resources.description: This PDF guide highlights easy ways to add math to daily routines like cooking, laundry, and playtime.
resources.instructions: Read through the guide and choose one idea to try this week. Post it on the fridge as a reminder.
objectives: Parents will gain practical ideas to support math at home.

resources.title: Online Pattern Builder Game
resources.summary: An interactive drag-and-drop game for creating and extending patterns.
resources.description: Children drag shapes to continue or create their own repeating patterns.
resources.instructions: Open the game on a tablet or computer and invite your child to drag shapes to complete and create patterns.
objectives: Children will identify and extend repeating patterns.

resources.title: Shape Scavenger Hunt Cards (Draft)
resources.summary: Draft printable cards for a shape scavenger hunt.
resources.description: Work-in-progress set of scavenger hunt cards for finding shapes around the house.
objectives: Children will identify shapes in their environment.
